EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built into Southern New England resulting in temperatures nearing 100 with high humidity. Heat index values ranged from 100 to 106 for most of Southern New England on the 6th and again on the 7th in a more limited area, generally the Connecticut River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values at the Nashua Boire Field (KASH) Automated Weather Observing System were 100 to 104 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm passing southern New England just southeast of Nantucket resulted in a period of snow across northern Massachusetts and southwest New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure moved across Southern New England bringing unseasonably warm air into the area. This allowed for good mixing which brought strong winds from a strong low level jet to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Manchester, firefighters responded to about 50 calls of downed trees and limbs that brought down power lines.  A tree fell onto a horse on a farm on Speare Road in Hudson.  A tree was downed onto a house in Merrimack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built into Southern New England resulting in temperatures nearing 100 with high humidity. Heat index values ranged from 100 to 106 for most of Southern New England on the 6th and again on the 7th in a more limited area, generally the Connecticut River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values at the Manchester Airport (KMHT) Automated Surface Observing System and the Nashua Boire Field (KASH) Automated Weather Observing System were 100 to 104 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening winter storm passed southeast of Nantucket and brought heavy snow and strong winds to southwest New Hampshire, resulting in near blizzard conditions at times.  More than 2000 flights were cancelled along the east coast due to the storm, despite this, Manchester Airport remained open through the storm.  Snowfall totals of 8 to 14 inches were widely observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were observed in eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure centered over Martha's Vineyard brought strong northeast winds onshore into southern New Hampshire. Sustained wind speeds were around 45 mph with gusts between 55 mph and 65 mph. Trees, utility poles, and wires were downed by these winds with these sometimes damaging homes, cars, and other property on the ground.  This also resulted in numerous power outages across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Manchester Airport (KMHT) recorded sustained wind speeds of 43 mph and gusts to 63 mph.  The wind downed numerous trees and wires across Bedford.  In addition, a scout camp near Manchester (Camp Carpenter) reported around 200 downed trees on its property.  They also sustained damage to many of their buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeastward from the New Jersey coast, southeast of Nantucket, and into the Gulf of Maine. This spread three to six inches of snow across much of Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.  Snow began in earnest around the afternoon rush hour.  This resulted in roads that were difficult to plow and thus slippery, resulting in numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system (surface low and upper level low on top of each other) moved southeast of Nantucket, spreading rain across Southern New England. This resulted in widespread rainfall totals of three to six inches. In eastern Massachusetts, a strong southeasterly low level jet pumped ample moisture into the area, resulting in rainfall totals on the order of six to ten inches. This resulted in major flooding across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, including small stream, urban, and poor drainage flooding. In addition, the Concord River at Lowell, the Shawsheen River at Wilimington, and the Pawtuxet River at Cranston reached record flood stages within two to four days of the rain. ||Strong winds associated with the low pressure system and the low level jet affected both the east and south coasts, resulting in numerous downed trees and wires and some minor structural damage to a few buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 42 year old man travelling on Interstate 93 in Manchester was killed when his car was struck by a 70 foot pine tree.  Two passengers, the man's 36 year old wife and 2 year old daughter, were injured and were transported to the hospital.  Saturated soil and strong winds combined, resulting in many downed trees across southern New Hampshire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England bringing strong winds, resulting in downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rindge, a large tree and wires were downed on Bancroft Road.  A tree and wires were downed on Murdough Hill Road in Nelson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England bringing strong winds, resulting in downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and wires were downed on Route 136 in Francestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises across Southern New England resulted in strong winds that brought down trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Route 31 in Antrim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm passing southern New England just southeast of Nantucket resulted in a period of snow across northern Massachusetts and southwest New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to nine inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 15th moved east to near Long Island by the morning of the 16th and then northeast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 17th.  The storm brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across Sullivan, Merrimack, and Rockingham Counties where snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

Strong coastal low pressure produced rainfall of 5 to 8 inches resulting in county wide flooding of small rivers and streams across numerous counties in New Hampshire. Road washouts were widespread with major damage occurring to roadways. Homes and businesses were also flooded. Total flood damages amounted to almost 8 million dollars across 6 counties, mainly in southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused county wide flooding of small rivers and streams in Coos County New Hampshire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced 1.5 to 2.5 inches of precipitation which caused minor flooding on the Saco River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Saco River at Conway (flood stage 9.0 ft) which crested at 9.9 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees in Coos and Grafton Counties in New Hampshire on the 1st of December. A wind gust of 56 mph was measured by a trained spotter in Whitefield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees and wires in Landaff Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees in Coos and Grafton Counties in New Hampshire on the 1st of December. A wind gust of 56 mph was measured by a trained spotter in Whitefield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees on Carriage Lane in Hanover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough located north to south from near Jackman Maine to the seacoast of New Hampshire served as a focus for multicell thunderstorm initiation. The storms were pulse severe and moved slowly, especially initially, likely causing some flooding problems underneath the heavier showers. Most spotters reported wind damage with these storms but some large hail was also produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a large tree which went through the roof of a house on Estes Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed power lines 4 miles west of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened to 993 mb off the New England coastline during a period of high astronomical tides.  This produced gale force winds along the New Hampshire seacoast.  This system produced 12 foot near-shore waves and a storm surge of over a foot on strong northeasterly gales.  Minor flooding occurred along low lying roads in Hampton, New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with gale force winds to produce minor coastal flooding along the New Hampshire sea coast. Minor flooding occurred along low lying roads in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along the eastern Massachusetts coast brought snow to much of interior Southern New England, with rain falling along the coast. Nine to twelve inch snowfall totals were widespread across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to sixteen inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.  The weight of the snow resulted in downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving very slowly westward through the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to northern New Hampshire. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the Connecticut River Valley to well over a foot in Coos County.

An area of low pressure developed over the southeastern states and then moved rapidly northeastward through the Gulf of Maine on the 18th.The storm brought heavy snow to the southern half of New Hampshire.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across much of the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 5 to 8 inches resulted in county wide fllooding of small rivers and streams in Rockingham County. This was the hardest hit area in New Hampshire with damages totaling over 4 million dollars.

An intense storm was slowly weakening over southern New England on the morning of February 26th.  Long period swells were slow to subside and some minor flooding occurred in Hampton at the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense storm was slowly weakening over southern New England on the morning of February 26th.  Long period swells were slow to subside and some minor flooding occurred in Hampton at the time of high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure over the southern New England coastline produced a modest storm surge and long period swells.  In Hampton, minor flooding occurred on low lying roads during the morning high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure over southern New England produced a modest storm surge and long period swell.  Minor flooding occurred in Hampton on low lying roads during the morning high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm system affected the northeastern U.S. from February 23rd through March 2nd.   Areas of surface low pressure rotating around an upper level atmospheric system brought high winds, heavy snow, heavy rain, inland and coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the area during the period.  ||On Monday, February 22nd, an upper level trough moved east from the Midwest. As the upper level system approached the East Coast on Tuesday, the 23rd, an area of surface low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast and moved northeast to east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 24th.  As this low moved northeast, a very strong and moist southeast flow developed between the low and a large area of high pressure over southeastern Canada.   The initial surface low hooked to the west as it rotated around the upper level low pressure system and gradually weakened.||By the morning of the 25th, a second surface low developed off the east coast and rotated around the upper level system as it intensified rapidly during the day.  Like the first surface low, the pressure gradient between high pressure over southeastern Canada and the intensifying low brought a continuation of the high winds, heavy rain, heavy snow, coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the northeast.   The high winds brought down trees and branches throughout the area and caused widespread power outages and blocked many roadways.  In addition, flooding damaged and/or closed many roads.  Along the coast, the strong east to northeast winds caused tides to surge which led to coastal flooding in the flood-prone areas.  In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across the state fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.    Snowfall across New Hampshire ranged from nothing along the immediate coast to 12 to 24 inches in Sullivan County and up to 24 to 48 inches in northern Grafton and southern Coos County.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||Total liquid-equivalent for the storm generally ranged from 4 to more than 6 inches in Rockingham and Strafford Counties, 3 to 6 inches across Carroll, Hillsborough, and Merrimack Counties, and 2 to 4 inches across Belknap, Cheshire, Sullivan and Grafton Counties, with lesser amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable across Coos County with values ranging from less than an inch in the Connecticut River Valley to more than 6 inches in the White Mountains.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state with the strongest winds and gusts generally in the southern part of the state, but also dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southeastern NH likely saw gusts in the 50 to 90 mph range.  The strongest gust reported was 94 mph at the Seabrook Nuclear Facility and a gust of 90 mph was recorded at the Isles of Shoals.  Across the state, wind gust reports were generally in the 45 to 65-mph range.   Official reported peak gusts included 94 mph at Seabrook, 68 mph at Portsmouth, 68 mph at Concord, 63 mph at Manchester, 49 mph at Laconia, 48 mph at Whitefield, 45 mph at Lebanon, 45 mph at Plymouth, and 44 mph at Jaffrey, 43 mph at Jaffrey, and 39 mph at Berlin.

The strongest gust reported was 94 mph at the Seabrook Nuclear Facility and a gust of 90 mph was recorded at the Isles of Shoals.  Across the state, wind gust reports were generally in the 45 to 65-mph range.   Official reported peak gusts included 94 mph at Seabrook, 68 mph at Portsmouth, 68 mph at Concord, 63 mph at Manchester, 49 mph at Laconia, 48 mph at Whitefield, 45 mph at Lebanon, 45 mph at Plymouth, and 44 mph at Jaffrey, 43 mph at Jaffrey, and 39 mph at Berlin.

A major storm system affected the northeastern U.S. from February 23rd through March 2nd.   Areas of surface low pressure rotating around an upper level atmospheric system brought high winds, heavy snow, heavy rain, inland and coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the area during the period.  ||On Monday, February 22nd, an upper level trough moved east from the Midwest. As the upper level system approached the East Coast on Tuesday, the 23rd, an area of surface low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast and moved northeast to east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 24th.  As this low moved northeast, a very strong and moist southeast flow developed between the low and a large area of high pressure over southeastern Canada.   The initial surface low hooked to the west as it rotated around the upper level low pressure system and gradually weakened.||By the morning of the 25th, a second surface low developed off the east coast and rotated around the upper level system as it intensified rapidly during the day.  Like the first surface low, the pressure gradient between high pressure over southeastern Canada and the intensifying low brought a continuation of the high winds, heavy rain, heavy snow, coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the northeast.   The high winds brought down trees and branches throughout the area and caused widespread power outages and blocked many roadways.  In addition, flooding damaged and/or closed many roads.  Along the coast, the strong east to northeast winds caused tides to surge which led to coastal flooding in the flood-prone areas.  In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across the state fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.    Snowfall across New Hampshire ranged from nothing along the immediate coast to 12 to 24 inches in Sullivan County and up to 24 to 48 inches in northern Grafton and southern Coos County.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||Total liquid-equivalent for the storm generally ranged from 4 to more than 6 inches in Rockingham and Strafford Counties, 3 to 6 inches across Carroll, Hillsborough, and Merrimack Counties, and 2 to 4 inches across Belknap, Cheshire, Sullivan and Grafton Counties, with lesser amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable across Coos County with values ranging from less than an inch in the Connecticut River Valley to more than 6 inches in the White Mountains.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state with the strongest winds and gusts generally in the southern part of the state, but also dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southeastern NH likely saw gusts in the 50 to 90 mph range.  The strongest gust reported was 94 mph at the Seabrook Nuclear Facility and a gust of 90 mph was recorded at the Isles of Shoals.  Across the state, wind gust reports were generally in the 45 to 65-mph range.   Official reported peak gusts included 94 mph at Seabrook, 68 mph at Portsmouth, 68 mph at Concord, 63 mph at Manchester, 49 mph at Laconia, 48 mph at Whitefield, 45 mph at Lebanon, 45 mph at Plymouth, and 44 mph at Jaffrey, 43 mph at Jaffrey, and 39 mph at Berlin.

An area of low pressure moving very slowly westward through the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to northern New Hampshire. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the Connecticut River Valley to well over a foot in Coos County.

An area of low pressure moving very slowly westward through the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to northern New Hampshire. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the Connecticut River Valley to well over a foot in Coos County.

Strong coastal low pressure resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain and caused moderate flooding on the Suncook and Lamprey Rivers in southern New Hampshire. The Lamprey River at Newmarket remained in flood through the end of the month. Total damages in Newmarket and on the Suncook River at Chichester amounted to well over half a million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester, (flood stage 7.0 ft). The river crested at 11.34 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeastward from the New Jersey coast, southeast of Nantucket, and into the Gulf of Maine. This spread three to six inches of snow across much of Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeastward from the New Jersey coast, southeast of Nantucket, and into the Gulf of Maine. This spread three to six inches of snow across much of Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through central New England on the 6th of June with thunderstorms breaking out ahead of an associated warm front in southern New Hampshire. Very strong wind fields associated with this system were responsible for several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees blocking Jenness Road in Epping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through central New England on the 6th of June with thunderstorms breaking out ahead of an associated warm front in southern New Hampshire. Very strong wind fields associated with this system were responsible for several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through central New England on the 6th of June with thunderstorms breaking out ahead of an associated warm front in southern New Hampshire. Very strong wind fields associated with this system were responsible for several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed tree branches on power lines in Londonderry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front crossed New Hampshire from northwest to southeast on the afternoon of June 20th. Several reports of wind damage were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Route 2 near the Lancaster/Jefferson line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front crossed New Hampshire from northwest to southeast on the afternoon of June 20th. Several reports of wind damage were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed 4 to 6 inch trees and carried lawn furniture 300 yards near Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a trailing cold front crossed the region on the afternoon of June 3rd. Thunderstorms broke out ahead of this system with some of them becoming severe across southeast New Hampshire. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds through the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Portsmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a trailing cold front crossed the region on the afternoon of June 3rd. Thunderstorms broke out ahead of this system with some of them becoming severe across southeast New Hampshire. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds through the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several large trees on Bunker Hill Road and Portsmouth Avenue in Statham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on several New Hampshire rivers at the end of March which lasted into April. Flooding was generally minor with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of March caused flooding on the Contoocook River at Henniker (flood stage 12.0 ft) which crested at 13.0 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on several New Hampshire rivers at the end of March which lasted into April. Flooding was generally minor with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of March caused flooding on the Lamprey River at Newmarket (flood stage 8.0 ft.) which crested at 12.6 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a weakening cold front to initiate showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. These storms produced damaging winds, downing trees, wires, and power poles throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a house on Peterborough Street in Jaffrey.  In Fitzwilliam, a tree fell onto a car on West Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on several New Hampshire rivers at the end of March which lasted into April. Flooding was generally minor with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of March caused flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester (flood stage 7.0 ft) which crested at 11.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system sat just south of Long Island for two days, bringing heavy rain to much of Southern New England during that time. A persistent southerly low level jet brought very moist air into the area, which resulted in high rainfall rates. A coastal front along the I-95 corridor enhanced rainfall in that area. This event followed a heavy rainfall and record flooding event in mid-March as well as a second lesser rain event about a week prior. Rivers across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island were still high from those events and warm temperatures in northern Vermont and New Hampshire resulted in a period of snowmelt, that resulted in rises on both the mainstem Connecticut and Merrimack Rivers. All of these factors led to a second record rainfall and flooding event. ||Two day rainfall totals across Southern New England ranged from an inch to ten inches. Though concentrated in Rhode Island and southeastern Massachusetts, all of Southern New England was affected by the flooding. In New Hampshire, two day rainfall totals ranged from three to four inches. Providence, Boston, and Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA set record monthly precipitation totals during the month of March. Providence also set the record for the wettest month ever in the period of record. Both the Pawtuxet River in Rhode Island and the Sudbury River in Massachusetts set floods of record. In mid to late May, a federal disaster was declared for Hillsborough County for the flooding in mid to late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain fell in Hillsborough County, resulting in several rivers going into minor flood, including the Contoocook at Peterborough, the Piscataquog at Goffstown, the Souhegan at Merrimack, and the Merrimack at Goffs Falls and at Nashua.  Several streets were flooded in Wilton and Hudson.||Rain running off High Street down a steep embankment into the Souhegan River in Greenville caused a mudslide that uprooted several trees and dislodged large boulders.||Damage amounts can be found in the March Storm Data when most of the damage occurred.  Rivers and small streams remained in flood into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a weakening cold front to initiate showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. These storms produced damaging winds, downing trees, wires, and power poles throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds on Turkey Hill Road, Island Drive, and Daniel Webster Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in several New Hampshire towns in Rockingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Londonderry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in several New Hampshire towns in Rockingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Windham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in several New Hampshire towns in Rockingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.5 inch hail in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of tropical storm Nicole interacted with a cold front resulting in 3 to 4 inches of rain in the Pemigewasset River Basin. Only minor flooding resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River (flood stage 9.0 ft) which crested at 9.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a fire in a small horse barn that also housed a tractor.  The horse stabled at the barn was outside at the time but the tractor was heavily damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a fire in a small horse barn that also housed a tractor.  The horse stabled at the barn was outside at the time but the tractor was heavily damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough located north to south from near Jackman Maine to the seacoast of New Hampshire served as a focus for multicell thunderstorm initiation. The storms were pulse severe and moved slowly, especially initially, likely causing some flooding problems underneath the heavier showers. Most spotters reported wind damage with these storms but some large hail was also produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough located north to south from near Jackman Maine to the seacoast of New Hampshire served as a focus for multicell thunderstorm initiation. The storms were pulse severe and moved slowly, especially initially, likely causing some flooding problems underneath the heavier showers. Most spotters reported wind damage with these storms but some large hail was also produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree and several branches on power lines in Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough located north to south from near Jackman Maine to the seacoast of New Hampshire served as a focus for multicell thunderstorm initiation. The storms were pulse severe and moved slowly, especially initially, likely causing some flooding problems underneath the heavier showers. Most spotters reported wind damage with these storms but some large hail was also produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed power lines 2 miles southeast of Lee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought showers and thunderstorms in southern New England. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds. One report of large hail was received from north central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds on Goddard Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure developed in the Gulf States and intensified as it moved from the coast of the Carolinas on the morning of the 26th, to Cape Cod on the morning of the 27th, and to northeastern Nova Scotia by the morning of the 28th.  Heavy snow fell across all of New Hampshire with amounts generally totaling about 6 to 16 inches with locally higher amounts.  Alexandria reported 18.5 inches from the storm.  In addition to heavy snow, the intense storm brought near blizzard conditions to the state.

A persistent and quasi-stationary area of low pressure near northern Maine brought upslope snows to the mountains of northern Coos County on the 6th and 7th of the month.  Snowfall amounts from about 6 to about 10 inches during the two days with most of the snow falling during the day on the 6th.
A cold front moved through southern New England, returning as a warm front the following day.  Storms fired along the warm front, producing significant wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front edging into the southern portions of Southern New England, followed by a cold front moving eastward across the area resulted in severe thunderstorms across much of the area. Many of these produced substantial wind damage and one storm produced an intermittent track F1 tornado that moved through northwestern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires along Route 123 were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front edging into the southern portions of Southern New England, followed by a cold front moving eastward across the area resulted in severe thunderstorms across much of the area. Many of these produced substantial wind damage and one storm produced an intermittent track F1 tornado that moved through northwestern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires on Deering Center Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front edging into the southern portions of Southern New England, followed by a cold front moving eastward across the area resulted in severe thunderstorms across much of the area. Many of these produced substantial wind damage and one storm produced an intermittent track F1 tornado that moved through northwestern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, including one onto wires and another onto a house.  An unknown amount of damage was done to the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck two buildings in Rockingham County on Thursday evening, August 5.  In Exeter, lightning ignited a fire in a 20 ft by 20 ft shed.  The shed and all its contents were destroyed.  In North Hampton, lightning struck the ground and followed metal piping and downspouts to a home where it blew a 3 ft diameter hole in the wall.

Lightning struck two buildings in Rockingham County on Thursday evening, August 5.  In Exeter, lightning ignited a fire in a 20 ft by 20 ft shed.  The shed and all its contents were destroyed.  In North Hampton, lightning struck the ground and followed metal piping and downspouts to a home where it blew a 3 ft diameter hole in the wall.

A strong upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, producing showers and thunderstorms across the area. Very cold air at the mid levels aided in producing large hail up to ping pong ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Parker Road were downed by thunderstorm winds, resulting in a temporary closure of Parker Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, producing showers and thunderstorms across the area. Very cold air at the mid levels aided in producing large hail up to ping pong ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covered Mount William Pond Road and Route 114 to a depth of one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, producing showers and thunderstorms across the area. Very cold air at the mid levels aided in producing large hail up to ping pong ball size.
Strong pressure rises across Southern New England resulted in strong winds that brought down trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Old Homestead Highway in Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure developed in the Gulf States and intensified as it moved from the coast of the Carolinas on the morning of the 26th, to Cape Cod on the morning of the 27th, and to northeastern Nova Scotia by the morning of the 28th.  Heavy snow fell across all of New Hampshire with amounts generally totaling about 6 to 16 inches with locally higher amounts.  Alexandria reported 18.5 inches from the storm.  In addition to heavy snow, the intense storm brought near blizzard conditions to the state.

Low pressure east of Nantucket resulted in a long duration snow storm across southern New Hampshire.  Snow fell on a near continuous basis for 24 to 48 hours.  Snow accumulated quickly across western and central Hillsborough County, resulting in nearly 8 inches of snow in the first 12 hours.  Snow fell at a slower speed across the rest of southwestern New Hampshire taking 24 to 36 hours to reach 6-8 inches.

Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam to form on the Ammonoosuc River resulting in minor flooding. The ice did damage to the covered bridge in Bath when 3 timber support beams and some floor support beams were damaged. The bridge was closed for a number of days. About 1/2 a mile of Abbott Avenue was damage due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Ammonoosuc River at Bath, resulting in minor flooding and damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam to form resulting in minor flooding on the Saco River in Conway. The Route 113 bridge in Fryeburg was closed for a time due to an ice jam that formed just upstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Saco River on the Route 113 bridge in Fryeburg, resulting in minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Sugar River in West Claremont, resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Sugar River in West Claremont, (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 9.51 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along the eastern Massachusetts coast brought snow to much of interior Southern New England, with rain falling along the coast. Nine to twelve inch snowfall totals were widespread across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to eighteen inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.  The weight of the snow resulted in downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong coastal low pressure resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain and caused moderate flooding on the Suncook and Lamprey Rivers in southern New Hampshire. The Lamprey River at Newmarket remained in flood through the end of the month. Total damages in Newmarket and on the Suncook River at Chichester amounted to well over half a million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused moderate flooding on the Lamprey River at Newmarket, (flood stage 8.0 ft). The river crested at 12.31 ft. but remained in flood into early March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm, (971 mb), retrograded towards the New England coastline.  This storm produced hurricane force winds with peak wind gusts of 91 mph at the Isles of Shoals.  This resulted in near-shore waves as high as 32 feet.  Significant splash-over occurred which produced flooding in low lying areas along the coast. In Hampton rocks and other debris as well as flooding closed down Brown avenue and other side streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense storm retrograded towards the New England coastline.  This storm produced hurricane force winds with peak wind gusts of 91 mph at the Isles of Shoals.  This resulted in near-shore waves as high as 32 feet.  Significant splash-over occurred which produced flooding in low lying coastal areas.  In Hampton, rocks and other debris as well as flooding closed down Brown avenue and other side streets.

In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across the state fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.    Snowfall across New Hampshire ranged from nothing along the immediate coast to 12 to 24 inches in Sullivan County and up to 24 to 48 inches in northern Grafton and southern Coos County.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||Total liquid-equivalent for the storm generally ranged from 4 to more than 6 inches in Rockingham and Strafford Counties, 3 to 6 inches across Carroll, Hillsborough, and Merrimack Counties, and 2 to 4 inches across Belknap, Cheshire, Sullivan and Grafton Counties, with lesser amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable across Coos County with values ranging from less than an inch in the Connecticut River Valley to more than 6 inches in the White Mountains.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state with the strongest winds and gusts generally in the southern part of the state, but also dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southeastern NH likely saw gusts in the 50 to 90 mph range.  The strongest gust reported was 94 mph at the Seabrook Nuclear Facility and a gust of 90 mph was recorded at the Isles of Shoals.  Across the state, wind gust reports were generally in the 45 to 65-mph range.   Official reported peak gusts included 94 mph at Seabrook, 68 mph at Portsmouth, 68 mph at Concord, 63 mph at Manchester, 49 mph at Laconia, 48 mph at Whitefield, 45 mph at Lebanon, 45 mph at Plymouth, and 44 mph at Jaffrey, 43 mph at Jaffrey, and 39 mph at Berlin.

Low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the morning of the 25th and deepened as it moved north, reaching southern New England by evening. Ahead of the low, east winds rapidly increased across New Hampshire with numerous gusts in excess of 60 mph being reported. A record number of homes and businesses lost power. Utilities reported 310,000 customers without power during the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 68 mph was measured by the Concord Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm system affected the northeastern U.S. from February 23rd through March 2nd.   Areas of surface low pressure rotating around an upper level atmospheric system brought high winds, heavy snow, heavy rain, inland and coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the area during the period.  ||On Monday, February 22nd, an upper level trough moved east from the Midwest. As the upper level system approached the East Coast on Tuesday, the 23rd, an area of surface low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast and moved northeast to east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 24th.  As this low moved northeast, a very strong and moist southeast flow developed between the low and a large area of high pressure over southeastern Canada.   The initial surface low hooked to the west as it rotated around the upper level low pressure system and gradually weakened.||By the morning of the 25th, a second surface low developed off the east coast and rotated around the upper level system as it intensified rapidly during the day.  Like the first surface low, the pressure gradient between high pressure over southeastern Canada and the intensifying low brought a continuation of the high winds, heavy rain, heavy snow, coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the northeast.   The high winds brought down trees and branches throughout the area and caused widespread power outages and blocked many roadways.  In addition, flooding damaged and/or closed many roads.  Along the coast, the strong east to northeast winds caused tides to surge which led to coastal flooding in the flood-prone areas.  In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across the state fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.    Snowfall across New Hampshire ranged from nothing along the immediate coast to 12 to 24 inches in Sullivan County and up to 24 to 48 inches in northern Grafton and southern Coos County.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||Total liquid-equivalent for the storm generally ranged from 4 to more than 6 inches in Rockingham and Strafford Counties, 3 to 6 inches across Carroll, Hillsborough, and Merrimack Counties, and 2 to 4 inches across Belknap, Cheshire, Sullivan and Grafton Counties, with lesser amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable across Coos County with values ranging from less than an inch in the Connecticut River Valley to more than 6 inches in the White Mountains.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state with the strongest winds and gusts generally in the southern part of the state, but also dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southeastern NH likely saw gusts in the 50 to 90 mph range.  The strongest gust reported was 94 mph at the Seabrook Nuclear Facility and a gust of 90 mph was recorded at the Isles of Shoals.  Across the state, wind gust reports were generally in the 45 to 65-mph range.   Official reported peak gusts included 94 mph at Seabrook, 68 mph at Portsmouth, 68 mph at Concord, 63 mph at Manchester, 49 mph at Laconia, 48 mph at Whitefield, 45 mph at Lebanon, 45 mph at Plymouth, and 44 mph at Jaffrey, 43 mph at Jaffrey, and 39 mph at Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening winter storm passed southeast of Nantucket and brought heavy snow and strong winds to southwest New Hampshire, resulting in near blizzard conditions at times.  More than 2000 flights were cancelled along the east coast due to the storm, despite this, Manchester Airport remained open through the storm.  Snowfall totals of 8 to 14 inches were widely observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 10 to 14 inches were observed in Cheshire County.

More than 2000 flights were cancelled along the east coast due to the storm, despite this, Manchester Airport remained open through the storm.  Snowfall totals of 8 to 14 inches were widely observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 7 to 12 inches were observed in western and central Hillsborough County. Strong winds brought down a utility pole in Wilton on Route 31 near Pleasant Street, temporarily closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved northeast out of the Ohio River valley spreading heavy rain and warm temperatures into the region. Temperatures warmed into the 50s with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall. The combination of heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding on the Ammonoosuc River at Bethlehem (flood stage 8.0 ft) which crested at 8.2 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved northeast out of the Ohio River valley spreading heavy rain and warm temperatures into the region. Temperatures warmed into the 50s with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall. The combination of heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding on the Saco River at Conway (flood stage 9.0 ft) which crested at 10.8 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure approached New England from the west on the 28th and stalled over the region on the 29th. A second area of low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the 29th and intensified as it moved north on the 30th. Low pressure turned out to sea passing through the Gulf of Maine late on the 30th and 31st. The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southern New Hampshire on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread flooding of small rivers and streams throughout Rockingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Carolinas on March 13 and moved northeast spreading heavy rain over New England. Rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches in southeast New Hampshire which caused serious flooding. Many areas saw county-wide damage especially in Rockingham County where there was over a million dollars in damage to property and infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Contoocook River at Henniker (flood stage 12.0 ft) which crested at 13.3 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure tracked north along the East Coast on the 23rd and then turned northeast, passing through the Gulf of Maine on the 24th. A tropical connection helped pump abundant moisture northward with rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches falling on already saturated ground. In addition, snow melt in the mountains contributed to rises on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with melting snow to produce brief minor flooding on the Androscoggin River at Gorham (flood stage 8.0 ft) which crested at 8.1 ft.

The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southern New Hampshire on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Saco River at Conway (flood stage 9.0) which crested at 9.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure approached New England from the west on the 28th and stalled over the region on the 29th. A second area of low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the 29th and intensified as it moved north on the 30th. Low pressure turned out to sea passing through the Gulf of Maine late on the 30th and 31st. The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southern New Hampshire on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Warner River at Davisville (flood stage 8.0 ft) which crested at 8.4 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An astronomical high tide combined with a weakening Nor'easter to produce coastal flooding and splash-over in Rockingham County. High water covered roads on Ashworth Avenue and Brown Street in Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderately large ocean waves from a weakening Nor'easter combined with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce flooding in coastal Rockingham County. High water covered roads on the west side of Ashworth and Brown streets in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Canadian Maritimes produced winds gusts of 40 to 50 mph throughout southern New England, resulting in scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by strong winds in Weare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Canadian Maritimes produced winds gusts of 40 to 50 mph throughout southern New England, resulting in scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought one tree down on Route 10 in Winchester. In Westmoreland, a tree was downed on Partridge Brook Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system sat just south of Long Island for two days, bringing heavy rain to much of Southern New England during that time. A persistent southerly low level jet brought very moist air into the area, which resulted in high rainfall rates. A coastal front along the I-95 corridor enhanced rainfall in that area. This event followed a heavy rainfall and record flooding event in mid-March as well as a second lesser rain event about a week prior. Rivers across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island were still high from those events and warm temperatures in northern Vermont and New Hampshire resulted in a period of snowmelt, that resulted in rises on both the mainstem Connecticut and Merrimack Rivers. All of these factors led to a second record rainfall and flooding event. ||Two day rainfall totals across Southern New England ranged from an inch to ten inches. Though concentrated in Rhode Island and southeastern Massachusetts, all of Southern New England was affected by the flooding. In New Hampshire, two day rainfall totals ranged from three to four inches.  Providence, Boston, and Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA set record monthly precipitation totals during the month of March. Providence also set the record for the wettest month ever in the period of record. Both the Pawtuxet River in Rhode Island and the Sudbury River in Massachusetts set floods of record.  In mid to late May, a federal disaster was declared for Hillsborough County for the flooding in mid to late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain fell in Hillsborough County, resulting in several rivers going into minor flood, including the Contoocook at Peterborough, the Piscataquog at Goffstown, the Souhegan at Merrimack, and the Merrimack at Goffs Falls and at Nashua.  Several streets were flooded in Wilton and Hudson.||Rain running off High Street down a steep embankment into the Souhegan River in Greenville caused a mudslide that uprooted several trees and dislodged large boulders.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure centered over Martha's Vineyard brought strong northeast winds onshore into southern New Hampshire. Sustained wind speeds were around 45 mph with gusts between 55 mph and 65 mph. Trees, utility poles, and wires were downed by these winds with these sometimes damaging homes, cars, and other property on the ground.  This also resulted in numerous power outages across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were downed throughout Wilton, Milford, Goffstown, and Amherst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through southern Maine and New Hampshire during the evening of May 4th triggered convection across southern New Hampshire. One of these cells produced damaging winds in Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm broke large branches off trees in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front moved southwest through the region during the evening of May 26th. Convection was initiated in a strongly capped environment and quickly turned severe. These storms produced mainly large hail as they moved out of Vermont and into southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a weakening cold front to initiate showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. These storms produced damaging winds, downing trees, wires, and power poles throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hinsdale: trees on Old Chesterfield Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  On Gateway Drive, a tree was downed onto a house.  In Winchester: trees on Tamarack Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  Trees were downed onto two homes, one on Snow Road and another on Forest Lake Road.  Another tree fell on a car on Keene Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a weakening cold front to initiate showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. These storms produced damaging winds, downing trees, wires, and power poles throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Route 10 and Cobble Hill Road were downed by thunderstorm winds, blocking the roads.  A utility pole was downed on Sawyers Crossing Road near the intersection of Old Eaton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system (surface low and upper level low on top of each other) moved southeast of Nantucket, spreading rain across Southern New England. This resulted in widespread rainfall totals of three to six inches. In eastern Massachusetts, a strong southeasterly low level jet pumped ample moisture into the area, resulting in rainfall totals on the order of six to ten inches. This resulted in major flooding across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, including small stream, urban, and poor drainage flooding. In addition, the Concord River at Lowell, the Shawsheen River at Wilimington, and the Pawtuxet River at Cranston reached record flood stages within two to four days of the rain. ||Strong winds associated with the low pressure system and the low level jet affected both the east and south coasts, resulting in numerous downed trees and wires and some minor structural damage to a few buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mainstem rivers went into flood, including the Contoocook River at Peterborough, the Piscataquog River at Goffstown and the Souhegan River at Merrimack.  In addition, several small streams overflowed their banks, flooding nearby roads in Merrimack, New Ipswich, Milford, and Amherst.||The Nashua River in Nashua flooded nearby parking lots and a crawlspace below a portrait studio.  In New Boston, a twenty foot section of Lyndeborough Road was washed out.  Several roads were closed, mainly in the Souhegan Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a weakening cold front to initiate showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. These storms produced damaging winds, downing trees, wires, and power poles throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, power poles, and wires were downed throughout Milford, including on Adams Road, Oak, King, Knight, and Prospect Streets.  Route 13 between North River Road and Patch Hill was closed while downed wires and power poles were repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A minor shortwave with a weak surface reflection moved across New York during the day, and into northern New England late in the afternoon. A storm cluster developed in Vermont, in the vicinity and south of a triple point, where dew points surged from mid 60s to low 70s in a small warm sector. These stroms progressed into central and southern New Hampshire during the late afternoon and evening hours. The majority of storms caused wind damage but some large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed 2 one foot diameter trees at Forest Brook and Pleasant View Roads near Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail. These storms produced several tornadoes later in the evening across the border in southwest Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in the town of Orford.  The storm snapped or uprooted hundreds of trees and downed numerous power poles along a continuous path in the area.  Winds were estimated to have gusted between 80 and 90 mph.  The majority of damage was along Grimes Hill Road and Bean Brook Road.  In addition, large hail damaged corn crops along Route 10 and Grimes Hill Road in Orford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail. These storms produced several tornadoes later in the evening across the border in southwest Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a severe thunderstorm downed trees in the towns of Wentworth and Dorchester.  Dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted in a non-continuous path.  Winds were estimated to have gusted between 80 and 90 mph.  The majority of the damage occurred along Town Line Pond Road, Dorchester Road, Bickford Woods Road, Route 118, and North Groton Roads. In addition, one person was injured when a tree fell on a vehicle in Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England, returning as a warm front the following day.  Storms fired along the warm front, producing significant wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick moving line of thunderstorms produced a series of microbursts that resulted in tremendous damage across a one and a half to two square mile area of Harrisville.  Numerous trees 12 to 24 inches in diameter were downed.  Structural damage appeared to be limited to approximately a dozen buildings.  One structure was severely damaged by a fallen tree on Eastside Road.  Another tree was downed onto the roof of a porch near Silver Lake and another destroyed a boat trailer at the same house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed multiple trees and wires on Route 118 in Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, producing showers and thunderstorms across the area. Very cold air at the mid levels aided in producing large hail up to ping pong ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covered Route 114 to about an inch in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought showers and thunderstorms in southern New England. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds. One report of large hail was received from north central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One woman and one boy were struck by lightning while on the beach at Lake Monomonoc. Newspaper reports indicated that they each suffered burns to their legs and feet and were transported to the hospital.  No information was available on the ages of the victims.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in southwest New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman took shelter from the storm in her garage on Royalston Road.  She was wet, barefoot and leaning against a metal tool chest when the lightning struck nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Nicole interacted with a cold front to produce 2 to 3 inches of rain in just a few hours and caused flash flooding of several roads and a mud slide that covered Route 3 in the town of Bath. Total damages in New Hampshire amounted to $75,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Bethlehem which washed out roads.

Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail. These storms produced several tornadoes later in the evening across the border in southwest Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Claremont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave approaching from the northwest and a prefrontal trough were the catalyst for severe thunderstorms and some super cells across northern New England  on the afternoon of June 5th. As morning convection associated with a warm front moved east of the area, strong surface heating and good low level shear combined to produced several long lived thunderstorms. One of these storms moving out of northern Vermont produced several reports of damaging winds and large hail in Coos County. The same storm produced a brief EF0 tornado in Gorham New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on Bunker Hill Road in Groveton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in southwest New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Peterborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in southwest New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were brought down in Weare, including one on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a trailing cold front crossed the region on the afternoon of June 3rd. Thunderstorms broke out ahead of this system with some of them becoming severe across southeast New Hampshire. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds through the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped a tree on a building on Old Post Road in Greenland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moving across southern Canada provided broad scale ascent and instability which led to the development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. Strong winds aloft (>50KT at 700mb) provided enough shear for organization and primarily a wind threat. There were numerous reports of wind damage from bow echoes and one supercell that moved across southeast New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped off trees at the intersection of Route 132 and 127 near Sanbornton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure approached New England from the west on the 28th and stalled over the region on the 29th. A second area of low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the 29th and intensified as it moved north on the 30th. Low pressure turned out to sea passing through the Gulf of Maine late on the 30th and 31st. The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southern New Hampshire on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Lamprey River in Newmarket (flood stage 8.0 ft) which remained above flood stage through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave approaching from the northwest and a prefrontal trough were the catalyst for severe thunderstorms and some super cells across northern New England  on the afternoon of June 5th. As morning convection associated with a warm front moved east of the area, strong surface heating and good low level shear combined to produced several long lived thunderstorms. One of these storms moving out of northern Vermont produced several reports of damaging winds and large hail in Coos County. The same storm produced a brief EF0 tornado in Gorham New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly near Brook Street in Gorham and did minor damage to two homes. In addition...the tornado snapped or uprooted several trees in the immediate area. Although the damage was minor...the rotational characteristics of the damage clearly indicated that a tornado had touched down. In addition...the funnel cloud and subsequent tornado was well-documented by nearby residents who captured both photographic and video evidence of the funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A minor shortwave with a weak surface reflection moved across New York during the day, and into northern New England late in the afternoon. A storm cluster developed in Vermont, in the vicinity and south of a triple point, where dew points surged from mid 60s to low 70s in a small warm sector. These stroms progressed into central and southern New Hampshire during the late afternoon and evening hours. The majority of storms caused wind damage but some large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees near Orford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England, returning as a warm front the following day.  Storms fired along the warm front, producing significant wind damage and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Turkey Hill Road and Iris Drive in East Merrimack were downed by thunderstorm winds.
